steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Check Statement II using SAS
steps.content:
Statement II gives $AC=DF$. Combine that with the information already given:
  • $AB=DE=7$
  • $\angle A=\angle D=50^\circ$
  • $AC=DF$
The equal angle is between the two equal sides in each triangle, so this is SAS congruence. Statement II is sufficient.

steps.title: Check Statement III using Law of Cosines
steps.content:
Statement III claims $BC = EF$. Let's apply the Law of Cosines to both triangles:

In $\triangle ABC$: $$BC^2 = AB^2 + AC^2 - 2 \cdot AB \cdot AC \cdot \cos(50^{\circ}) = 49 + AC^2 - 14 AC \cos(50^{\circ})$$
In $\triangle DEF$: $$EF^2 = DE^2 + DF^2 - 2 \cdot DE \cdot DF \cdot \cos(50^{\circ}) = 49 + DF^2 - 14 DF \cos(50^{\circ})$$
If $BC = EF$, then $BC^2 = EF^2$, which gives:
$$AC^2 - 14 AC \cos(50^{\circ}) = DF^2 - 14 DF \cos(50^{\circ})$$
Rearranging: $(AC - DF)(AC + DF - 14\cos(50^{\circ})) = 0$

This equation has two possible solutions: either $AC = DF$ OR $AC + DF = 14\cos(50^{\circ}) \approx 9.00$.

steps.title: Counterexample for Statement III
steps.content:
The second case ($AC + DF \neq AC = DF$) allows non-congruent triangles. For instance:

Suppose $\triangle ABC$ has angles $A = 50^{\circ}, B = 50^{\circ}, C = 80^{\circ}$ and $AB = 7$.
Suppose $\triangle DEF$ has angles $D = 50^{\circ}, E = 30^{\circ}, F = 100^{\circ}$ and $DE = 7$.

Using the Law of Sines, both triangles can be constructed with:
$$BC = \frac{7\sin(50^{\circ})}{\sin(80^{\circ})} \approx 5.45, \qquad EF = \frac{7\sin(50^{\circ})}{\sin(100^{\circ})} \approx 5.45$$
Since $\sin(80^{\circ}) = \sin(100^{\circ})$, these are equal. But $AC \approx 5.45$ (because $\triangle ABC$ is isosceles with $A = B = 50^{\circ}$) and $DF \approx 3.55$, so $AC \neq DF$.

These triangles satisfy all given conditions plus $BC = EF$, yet they are not congruent because their angles differ. Statement III is not sufficient.
